Christians who are familiar with the Scriptures may, depending humbly on Divine teaching, recognize those who teach doctrines according to the apostles and those who contradict them. The core of revealed religion is the doctrine concerning Christ, his person and office. False teachers spoke about the world according to its values and tastes, so as not to offend people who are devoted to physical pleasures. The world approved of them; they made rapid progress and had many followers like themselves. The world loves its own, and its own loves it. The true doctrine regarding the Savior's person, as leading people from the world to God, is a mark of the spirit of truth in opposition to the spirit of error. A doctrine is more likely to be from God if it is pure and holy; we cannot use any other rules to test whether spirits are from God or not. What is surprising is that people with worldly spirits cling to those who are like themselves, tailoring their plans and speeches to suit their corrupt tastes?

The Spirit of God is the spirit of love. Whoever does not love the image of God in his people has no true knowledge of God. For it is God’s nature to be kind and give happiness. The law of God is love; all would have been perfectly happy if everyone had obeyed it. The provision of the gospel, for forgiveness of sin and salvation of sinners, consistently with God's glory and justice, shows that God is love. Mystery and darkness rest upon many things yet. God has shown himself to be so loving that we cannot fail to achieve eternal happiness unless through unbelief and unrepentance; although strict justice would condemn us to hopeless misery because we break our Creator’s laws. No words or thoughts can do justice to the free, astonishing love of a holy God toward sinners, who could neither profit nor harm him, whom he might justly destroy in a moment, and whose deserving of his vengeance was shown in the way they were saved, though he could by his almighty Word have created other worlds with more perfect beings if he had chosen. If we search the entire universe for love in its most glorious displays, it is found in the person and cross of Christ. Does love exist between God and sinners? That was the origin—not that we loved God, but that he freely loved us. His love could not be designed to be fruitless; when its proper end and result are achieved, it may be said to be perfected. So faith is perfected by works. Thus, it will appear that God dwells in us through his new-creating Spirit. A loving Christian is a perfect Christian; set him to any good duty, and he is perfect at it; he is skilled at it. Love oils the wheels of his affections and sets him on what benefits his brothers. A man who does business with ill will always does it poorly. That God dwells in us and we in him were words too high for mortals to use if God had not put them before us. But how may it be known whether this testimony proceeds from the Holy Ghost? Those who are truly persuaded that they are sons of God cannot but call him Abba, Father. From love to him, they hate sin and whatever disagrees with his will, and they have a sincere desire to do his will. Such testimony is the testimony of the Holy Ghost.

The Father sent the Son; he willed his coming into this world. The apostle attests this. Whoever confesses that Jesus is the Son of God, God dwells in him, and he in God. This confession includes faith in the heart as its foundation; it makes acknowledgment with the mouth to the glory of God and Christ, and demonstrates profession in life and conduct, against the flattery and disapproval of the world. There must be a day of universal judgment. Happy those who will have holy boldness before the Judge at that day, knowing he is their Friend and Advocate! Happy those who have holy boldness in the prospect of that day, who look and wait for it and for the Judge’s appearance! True love to God assures believers of God's love to them. Love teaches us to suffer for him and with him; therefore we may trust that we also will be glorified with him. We must distinguish between the fear of God and being afraid of him; the fear of God means high regard and veneration for God. Obedience and good works, done from the principle of love, are not like the forced labor of one who unwillingly toils from dread of a master's anger. They are like that of a dutiful child, who does services for a beloved father, which benefit his brothers, and are done willingly. It is a sign that our love is far from perfect when we have many doubts, fears, and apprehensions about God. Let heaven and earth stand amazed at his love. He sent his word to invite sinners to partake of this great salvation. Let them take comfort in the happy change wrought in them while they give him the glory. The love of God in Christ, in the hearts of Christians from the Spirit of adoption, is the greatest proof of conversion. This must be tested by its effects on their temper and conduct toward their brothers. If a man professes to love God but indulges anger or revenge, or shows a selfish disposition, he contradicts his profession. But if it is plain that our natural hostility has changed into affection and gratitude, let us bless the name of our God for this seal and guarantee of eternal happiness. Then we differ from false professors who pretend to love God whom they have not seen but hate their brothers whom they have seen.
